m_style slips from the learner's board

p_stock slips from the learner's board
p_watermark slips from the learner's board

director end of act 6 overridden at 2 turns (min 3)
director end of act 7 overridden at 2 turns (min 3)
Liane of the Vara house composed the nocturne.

transcript.text: [The flooded archive gives back only damp silence, and the conservatory room gathers around an unsigned page as if around a verdict not yet spoken. Lamplight trembles over the table; outside, rain works at the windows.]

phase.name: Act 1
phase.intent: [The flooded archive gives back only damp silence, and the conservatory room gathers around an unsigned page as if around a verdict not yet spoken. Lamplight trembles over the table; outside, rain works at the windows.]

transcript.text: Before we name anyone, what would count as proof in this room: manner, opportunity, source, or the correcting hand? Which of those can an unsigned page give us first?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut is possible on the first turn; the erotema is conduct, not repetition.

transcript.text: The page calls for three things under Rule 6: opportunity, source, and the correcting hand. 'Corrected' is printed on the finding note — so the hand may be our first purchase. But without a watermark period, a ledger entry, or a quoted tune, opportunity and source are still dark. Vess is maestro, yes — but thirty years in a hall is not the same as being placed in a room on a specific date.

meta.hypothesis: Vess composed the nocturne — he presides over the conservatory and is the natural suspect — but this is ungrounded until we have couldHaveWritten, hadSourceOf, and draftCorrectedBy all established.

transcript.text: [The damp page remains under the lamp, its edges lifting and settling with the room's breath. No one moves to claim it; the rain keeps time against the glass.]
Good: you have named the law before the suspects. If the page tempts you toward a hand, what must the room first distinguish between a mere finding note and evidence that can bear Rule 6?

deliberation.diagnosis: Only one prior turn is on record, so a third consecutive use of erotema is impossible.

transcript.text: The finding note says 'corrected' — it tells us corrections exist on the page, not whose hand made them. That is a state, not an attribution. Rule 6's third limb requires 'draftCorrectedBy nocturne X' for a named X; 'corrected' alone fills no variable. Until ink or handwriting ties the striking-out to a person, the page shows only that someone rewrote — not who.

meta.hypothesis: Vess composed the nocturne — he presides over the conservatory and is the natural suspect — but this is ungrounded until we have couldHaveWritten, hadSourceOf, and draftCorrectedBy all established.

transcript.text: [Every musician shown the leaves says the same: this is Vess's late manner — the suspensions, the long pedal, the way the bass walks.]
Manner is a lantern, not a signature. It may show why the room looks toward Vess, but it has not placed him in the copy-room, given him the hidden tune, or put his hand in the corrections.

deliberation.diagnosis: The last two turns declared erotema and the draft declares erotema again, making a three-turn figure rut.
deliberation.note: Leave off erotema for this turn. Let the point arrive by contrast or statement, not another question.

transcript.text: Style gives us reason to look toward Vess, but no rule takes 'styleOf' as a premise — it feeds no chain, fills no variable. I adopt it as a shown fact, not as evidence. The three limbs of Rule 6 remain empty: no watermark period, no ledger entry, no quoted tune, no named hand in the corrections.

meta.hypothesis: Vess composed the nocturne — still ungrounded; 'styleOf' is not a legal premise under any of the six rules, and couldHaveWritten, hadSourceOf, and draftCorrectedBy remain unestablished.

transcript.text: [The lamp is turned a fraction lower, and the wet paper answers with a duller sheen. Around the table, certainty thins into waiting; the rain presses its fingers along the dark panes.]

Then let the style remain where you have put it: a reason to inquire, not a proof to conclude. If manner names a suspect but fills no limb, which limb should the room seek first: the time of writing, the source of the tune, or the correcting hand?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut: the last two turns were erotema and analogia, so this erotema does not make three in a row.

transcript.text: The time of writing is the foundation: without a period established by watermark or ink, Rule 3 cannot give us couldHaveWritten, and the ledger has no bracket to search. Settle the period first — it narrows the field to whoever signed the ledger then, and every subsequent limb must fall within that same window. The correcting hand is sharpest once suspects are narrowed; the quoted tune can follow either order.

meta.hypothesis: Vess composed the nocturne — still ungrounded; 'styleOf' is not a legal premise under any rule, and couldHaveWritten, hadSourceOf, and draftCorrectedBy remain unestablished. Seek watermark or ink batch first to open Rule 1 or 2, then Rule 3.

transcript.text: [The conservatory holds its breath while the leaves rest under the low lamp, their edges dark with old water. No one reaches for the signature that is not there; the room waits for the paper itself to answer.]
Then keep Vess in the margin, not at the center. If time is the first gate, what would the paper or ink have to prove before any ledger can matter?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut: the last two declared figures are analogia and erotema, so this would not make three erotemas in a row.

transcript.text: The paper must yield a watermark that can be matched to a stock used only during one period — that opens Rule 1 and gives us writtenDuring. Failing that, the ink must be identified as a batch mixed only during one period — that is Rule 2, the same gate by a different key. Either fact alone is enough to fix a period; without one of them, the ledger has no bracket and Rule 3 cannot fire. So the question for the next witness is plain: what stock is this, and when was it last in the copy-room?

meta.hypothesis: Vess composed the nocturne — still ungrounded; 'styleOf' is not a legal premise under any rule, and couldHaveWritten, hadSourceOf, and draftCorrectedBy remain unestablished. Seek watermark (Rule 1) or ink batch (Rule 2) to establish writtenDuring before the ledger can be searched.

transcript.text: [The page remains beneath the lamp, its blank upper corner paler than the drowned margins. A clerk sets an unopened folio beside it and withdraws; the room does not yet turn the cover.]

Then turn the leaves to the lamp: every sheet answers with the same heron watermark, the Brell mill's mark. The mark names the stock; only the stock-book can turn that name into a winter narrow enough for the law.

deliberation.diagnosis: The last two turns declared erotema and this draft declares erotema again, making a real three-turn figure rut.
deliberation.note: Leave erotema off this turn. State the pressure or consequence without casting it as another question.

transcript.text: The heron mark is one half of Rule 1 — I have the watermark, but the rule asks for stockUsedOnlyDuring as well. Without the stock-book's entry, I cannot fix a period, and the ledger remains unbounded. The next question is plain: when did heronStock enter and leave the copy-room?

meta.hypothesis: Vess composed the nocturne — still ungrounded; 'styleOf' is not a legal premise under any rule, and couldHaveWritten, hadSourceOf, and draftCorrectedBy remain unestablished. watermark is now confirmed as heronStock, but stockUsedOnlyDuring heronStock is not yet in evidence, so Rule 1 cannot fire and writtenDuring remains unestablished.

transcript.text: [The unopened folio waits beside the lamp, its cover swollen from the flood and buckled at the corners. Rain ticks against the windows while the room leans toward the book and says nothing.]
Good: the heron mark names the stock, not the season. If Rule 1 is a door with two bolts, which bolt has turned, and which is still waiting in the unopened folio?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut: the last two turns are erotema then analogia, and the draft only makes two analogia turns separated by an erotema.

transcript.text: The first bolt has turned: watermark nocturne heronStock is on my board. The second bolt — stockUsedOnlyDuring heronStock — still waits inside the folio. Until the stock-book is opened and read, Rule 1 cannot close and writtenDuring remains unestablished. The folio is the next witness.

meta.hypothesis: Vess composed the nocturne — still ungrounded; 'styleOf' is not a legal premise under any rule, and couldHaveWritten, hadSourceOf, and draftCorrectedBy remain unestablished. watermark is confirmed as heronStock (first bolt of Rule 1 turned), but stockUsedOnlyDuring heronStock is not yet in evidence (second bolt still in the unopened folio), so writtenDuring remains unestablished.

transcript.text: [The swollen folio lies unopened in the lamplight, its warped cover casting a narrow shadow across the unsigned page. The room shifts from verdict to reckoning; rain hardens at the windows as the next act gathers around time itself.]

phase.name: Act 2
phase.intent: [The swollen folio lies unopened in the lamplight, its warped cover casting a narrow shadow across the unsigned page. The room shifts from verdict to reckoning; rain hardens at the windows as the next act gathers around time itself.]

transcript.text: Before the folio speaks, keep the first fact steady: the nocturne's leaves bear the heron watermark, the Brell mill's mark. What, precisely, must the stock-book add before that mark becomes a season under Rule 1?

deliberation.diagnosis: The last two figures were analogia and the draft moves to erotema, so there is no three-turn figure rut.

transcript.text: The mark alone is not enough. Rule 1 needs two premises: the watermark fact — which I hold — and stockUsedOnlyDuring heronStock to some specific period. The stock-book must show that the heron stock entered and left the copy-room within one named span, and nothing else. Until that second premise is in evidence, writtenDuring remains unestablished and Rule 1 cannot fire.

meta.hypothesis: Vess composed the nocturne — still ungrounded; 'styleOf' is not a legal premise under any rule, and couldHaveWritten, hadSourceOf, and draftCorrectedBy remain unestablished. watermark is confirmed as heronStock (first bolt of Rule 1 in hand), but stockUsedOnlyDuring heronStock is not yet in evidence (second bolt still locked in the folio), so writtenDuring remains unestablished.

transcript.text: [The steward's stock-book is carried in with its swollen clasp broken and its entries read into the wet hush. The steward's stock-book is plain: heron paper entered the copy-room in the winter of the flood, and the last of it was spent before the thaw. None before, none since.]
Now the second bolt has turned. The heron mark names the stock; the stock-book names the flood winter and excludes every other season. Under Rule 1, what must that do to the nocturne's date?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut: the last two declared figures differ and the draft moves to anaphora, while its premise work matches the reconstruction.

transcript.text: The second bolt is now in hand — stockUsedOnlyDuring heronStock floodWinter is entered. But my board has a gap: watermark nocturne heronStock, grounded in a prior act, has slipped from it. Both premises are needed before Rule 1 fires; I will not derive writtenDuring on one leg. The stage confirms the heron mark; I name the gap aloud and ask that the first premise be returned to evidence.

meta.hypothesis: Vess composed the nocturne — still ungrounded; couldHaveWritten, hadSourceOf, and draftCorrectedBy remain unestablished. stockUsedOnlyDuring heronStock floodWinter is now on my board (second bolt of Rule 1), but watermark nocturne heronStock has slipped from my board (gap noted), so Rule 1 cannot yet fire and writtenDuring remains unvoiced.

transcript.text: [The lamp gutters over the open stock-book, throwing the unsigned leaves and the ledger into alternating bands of light. No one moves to close either volume; the rain keeps time against the panes while the room waits for the two pieces already present to be held together.]

Then set the leaf back under the lamp: every sheet of the nocturne bears the heron watermark, the Brell mill's mark. With that mark restored and the stock-book limiting heron paper to the flood winter, what does Rule 1 now force?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut: the last two figures are erotema and anaphora, and the draft's anaphora does restore the premise its reconstruction marks missing.

transcript.text: Both premises are on my board and the tutor's confirmation is explicit. Rule 1 fires now: watermark nocturne heronStock, stockUsedOnlyDuring heronStock floodWinter — therefore writtenDuring nocturne floodWinter. The date is settled. The person is not; I still need the ledger for Rule 3, a quoted melody for Rule 5, and the corrected draft for Rule 6.

meta.hypothesis: Vess composed the nocturne — still ungrounded; couldHaveWritten, hadSourceOf, and draftCorrectedBy remain unestablished. writtenDuring nocturne floodWinter is now derived (Rule 1), opening the path to Rule 3 via the ledger.

transcript.text: [The stock-book remains open beside the unsigned nocturne, its wet pages slowly flattening under glass weights. Beyond the windows the rain turns steadier, and the room holds its breath around the newly fixed season without yet naming what it costs.]

Hold the date apart from the name. Now that the nocturne is fixed to the flood winter, can Vess's manner by itself survive as a verdict, or only as a suspect's shadow awaiting opportunity under the rules?

deliberation.diagnosis: The last two turns declared anaphora and this draft declares anaphora again, so the figure has become a rut.
deliberation.note: Leave off anaphora for this turn. Let the pressure move without the repeated balancing frame.

transcript.text: Manner is not a premise under any of the six rules — it points but it cannot prove. styleOf nocturne vess names Vess as the natural suspect, nothing more; a shadow of style discharges none of the three required facts. writtenDuring is settled; couldHaveWritten, hadSourceOf, and draftCorrectedBy all remain open. Until the ledger places him at the conservatory in floodWinter, a melody binds him to a house, and his hand is found striking through the draft, the verdict cannot be given.

meta.hypothesis: Vess composed the nocturne — still ungrounded; styleOf is legally inert under all six rules, and couldHaveWritten, hadSourceOf, and draftCorrectedBy remain unestablished. writtenDuring nocturne floodWinter is derived (Rule 1), opening the path to Rule 3 via the ledger — that is the next required exhibit.

transcript.text: [A runner from the capital is shown in with a sodden packet of concert bills, each sheet pressed flat and read into the room. The capital's concert bills run unbroken through the flood winter: Vess played the Esten halls every week of it, and did not return until the thaw.]

The bills do not erase the style; they test what style was allowed to promise. If the nocturne belongs to the flood winter, and Vess was in the Esten halls through that winter, which limb of Rule 6 has his manner failed to fill?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut: the last two declared figures are mixed, so a second erotema is conduct rather than three in a row.

transcript.text: The failed limb is couldHaveWritten — the first of Rule 6's three conditions. Rule 3 requires atConservatoryDuring vess floodWinter, but the concert bills establish he was in Esten through the entire flood winter; that entry can never be made. The Vess hypothesis is no longer merely ungrounded — it is actively blocked: no one writes in a room they never entered. I must seek whoever did sign the copy-room ledger during flood winter; a different hand is required.

meta.hypothesis: Someone other than Vess composed the nocturne — a person who was at the conservatory during flood winter — but no such person has yet been named in evidence.

transcript.text: [The concert bills are left in a damp fan beside the folio, their ink drying into a hard, public alibi. The room grows quieter around the vacant place where a name should stand, while rain ticks from the coats hung by the door.]

Set the shadow beside the calendar and keep them separate. The style made Vess worth testing; the heron stock fixed the flood winter; the bills put him away through that winter. Let the next search follow the law of opportunity: someone must be placed in the conservatory during the period already proved.

deliberation.diagnosis: The draft makes a third consecutive erotema, so the question-form has become a figure rut.
deliberation.note: Leave off erotema this turn. Make the next move as a plain directive or measured statement, not another question.

transcript.text: The stage brings nothing new this turn — the board is unchanged. awayDuring vess floodWinter stands; the Vess hypothesis is not merely ungrounded but formally closed. The inquiry now waits on a single question: who signed the copy-room ledger during flood winter? Until a name is brought from that ledger, no Rule 3 chain can open and no attribution is possible.

meta.hypothesis: Someone who signed the copy-room ledger during flood winter composed the nocturne — but no such person has yet been named in evidence.

transcript.text: [The papers are gathered into separate piles under the lamp: one for likeness, one for time, one for absence. Beyond the rain-dark windows, the archive seems to listen for another kind of testimony, softer than ledgers and harder to dismiss.]

phase.name: Act 3
phase.intent: [The papers are gathered into separate piles under the lamp: one for likeness, one for time, one for absence. Beyond the rain-dark windows, the archive seems to listen for another kind of testimony, softer than ledgers and harder to dismiss.]

transcript.text: Call the night porter from the rain-dark passage. He remembers the flood winter well: the copy-room lamps burned past midnight, week upon week, and it was the archive girl, Liane, who kept them burning. If the flood winter is the period already proved, what does this testimony do to opportunity under Rule 3?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut: the recent sequence is erotema, anaphora, erotema, not three of one device.

transcript.text: The porter's account lands on stage as exhibited fact, not mere saying — I enter it. writtenDuring nocturne floodWinter was proved by watermark and stock period; Rule 3 now fires: atConservatoryDuring liane floodWinter yields couldHaveWritten liane nocturne. Vess was away that winter — awayDuring vess floodWinter closes his path to couldHaveWritten entirely; his hypothesis is dead. I still want hadSourceOf and draftCorrectedBy before the board settles the authorship.

meta.hypothesis: Liane composed the nocturne — she had opportunity (couldHaveWritten liane nocturne now derived) — but hadSourceOf liane nocturne and draftCorrectedBy nocturne liane remain unestablished and must be sought before Rule 6 can fire.

transcript.text: [The porter is shown back into the wet corridor, and the door closes softly behind him. Under the lamp, the vacant space beside opportunity remains unfilled, while the folio waits with its pages held open by two dull weights.]
Opportunity is now a filled square, not a verdict. If Liane could have written the nocturne in the flood winter, which two limbs of Rule 6 still stand empty before any name may be spoken?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut: the last two declared figures are anaphora then erotema, so this would not make three of the same device in a row.

transcript.text: The first limb is filled: couldHaveWritten liane nocturne holds under Rule 3, with writtenDuring and atConservatoryDuring both grounded. The two empty squares are hadSourceOf liane nocturne — Rule 5 would fill it if a quoted melody was kept within walls where Liane dwelled — and draftCorrectedBy nocturne liane, which only the draft's own corrections can settle. Rule 6 will not fire without both. I keep the question open.

meta.hypothesis: Liane composed the nocturne — she had opportunity (couldHaveWritten liane nocturne now derived) — but hadSourceOf liane nocturne and draftCorrectedBy nocturne liane remain unestablished and must be sought before Rule 6 can fire.

transcript.text: [Rain ticks steadily against the high windows while the lamp gutters once and steadies. The folio lies between the emptied witness chair and the unopened inner cabinet, its silence sharpened by the spaces the board has left blank.]

Keep the first square closed: Liane had opportunity in the flood winter. Treat the source square as a locked cabinet, not a rumor in the hall; it opens only when the room can show a work borrowing from something kept within a house, and a claimant dwelling under that roof.

deliberation.diagnosis: The declared figure would be erotema for a third consecutive turn, which is a real figure rut.
deliberation.note: Leave off erotema this turn. Make the move as a plain imperative or declarative pressure instead of another question.

transcript.text: No new exhibits land. The tutor confirms the first limb is filled — but I find a gap: stockUsedOnlyDuring heronStock floodWinter is missing from my board, so Rule 1 cannot formally fire from what is written here. I name the gap and ask it be restored. The two remaining limbs — hadSourceOf and draftCorrectedBy — stay open.

meta.hypothesis: Liane composed the nocturne — she had opportunity in the flood winter — but hadSourceOf liane nocturne and draftCorrectedBy nocturne liane remain unestablished and must be sought before Rule 6 can fire.

transcript.text: [The lamp is trimmed lower, and the piles on the table cast longer, more separate shadows. No hand reaches for the inner cabinet yet; the room holds its breath around the empty places on the board.]

First restore the slipped bolt: the steward's stock-book limited heron paper to the flood winter, none before, none since. Now play the nocturne once; its middle voice carries an older tune entire, a cradle-song set like a keepsake under the right hand's figures. For the source limb, what has this shown, and what kind of witness is still lacking before Rule 5 can speak?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut: the last two figures were erotema and analogia, and the draft's erotema also restores the reconstruction's missing premise before moving on.

transcript.text: The tutor restores the slipped bolt: stockUsedOnlyDuring heronStock floodWinter re-enters the board, and Rule 1 fires — writtenDuring nocturne floodWinter holds; Rule 3 follows at once: couldHaveWritten liane nocturne. Exhibit 0 turns Rule 5's first fastening: quotes nocturne cradleSong is now grounded. Two fastenings remain unturned: the stage must show heardOnlyWithin cradleSong ?place — that the cradleSong was sealed within one house, never printed nor performed abroad — and dwelledIn liane ?place, that Liane lived under that same roof. Until both land in evidence, hadSourceOf liane nocturne cannot be voiced.

meta.hypothesis: Liane composed the nocturne — couldHaveWritten liane nocturne is derived (Rule 3), and quotes nocturne cradleSong is now grounded (Rule 5 first premise) — but hadSourceOf liane nocturne still requires heardOnlyWithin cradleSong ?place and dwelledIn liane ?place, both absent; draftCorrectedBy nocturne liane is also unestablished. Rule 6 cannot fire without all three limbs.

transcript.text: [The needle is lifted from the worn cylinder and the last thread of the tune vanishes into the rain. On the table, the source pile remains open but unjoined, its blank space catching more lamplight than the pages around it.]
The cradle-song is a key, but not yet a door opened. Under Rule 5, what must the next testimony show about the tune's keeping, and what separate fact must place Liane within the same walls?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut: the last two figures were analogia and erotema, and the draft's erotema does not make three in a row.

transcript.text: Rule 5 wants two facts sharing a matched place name: heardOnlyWithin cradleSong ?place — testimony that the tune was sealed within one house, never printed nor performed abroad — and dwelledIn liane ?place, showing Liane lived under that same roof. Without both, and the same ?place in each, hadSourceOf liane nocturne cannot be voiced. I note further that stockUsedOnlyDuring heronStock floodWinter has slipped from my board again; I ask it be restored so writtenDuring nocturne floodWinter and couldHaveWritten liane nocturne can be formally re-derived rather than merely assumed.

meta.hypothesis: Liane composed the nocturne — couldHaveWritten liane nocturne was derived in prior turns (Rule 3) and quotes nocturne cradleSong is grounded (Rule 5 first premise) — but hadSourceOf liane nocturne still requires heardOnlyWithin cradleSong ?place and dwelledIn liane ?place with a matching ?place, both absent from the board; draftCorrectedBy nocturne liane is also unestablished. Rule 6 cannot fire without all three limbs.

transcript.text: [Rain fingers the tall windows while the lamp gutters and steadies over the separated piles. The open space beside the source papers remains untouched, as if the archive itself is waiting for a witness willing to enter it.]

Then set the stock-book back under glass: heron paper entered the copy-room in the flood winter, and the last of it was spent before the thaw — none before, none since. The leaf still bears the heron mark; the porter still places Liane in that winter. With time and opportunity restored, what two fastenings must the source limb still receive before Rule 5 can speak?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut: the last two turns used erotema, but this draft shifts to anaphora and also addresses the reconstructed missing stock premise.

transcript.text: The tutor restores the bolt; items 4 and 5 are both on my board, so Rule 1 fires and writtenDuring nocturne floodWinter stands — Rule 3 follows at once from item 7, giving couldHaveWritten liane nocturne. Rule 5 still wants two fastenings sharing a matched place: heardOnlyWithin cradleSong ?place — that the cradle-song was sealed within one house, never printed nor performed abroad — and dwelledIn liane ?place, that Liane lived under that same roof. Until both arrive, and with the same ?place in each, hadSourceOf liane nocturne cannot be voiced. draftCorrectedBy nocturne liane is also still outstanding; Rule 6 cannot fire until all three limbs are grounded.

meta.hypothesis: Liane composed the nocturne — couldHaveWritten liane nocturne is now derived (Rule 3), and quotes nocturne cradleSong is grounded (Rule 5 first premise) — but hadSourceOf liane nocturne still requires heardOnlyWithin cradleSong ?place and dwelledIn liane ?place with a matching ?place, both absent; draftCorrectedBy nocturne liane is also unestablished. Rule 6 cannot fire without all three limbs.

transcript.text: [Mistress Odile is shown in from the rain with her shawl still dark at the hem. She knows the tune at one hearing: the Vara cradle-song, never printed, never performed, sung only within that house's walls.]

Now the tune has walls: the Vara house, not the public hall. Rule 5 has the quoted cradle-song and the place that kept it; what separate fact about a candidate must share that same roof before source can be awarded?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut: the last two figures are erotema and anaphora, and the draft's erotema does not make three in a row.

transcript.text: The tune is sealed within the Vara house — heardOnlyWithin cradleSong varaHouse is now grounded. Rule 5 has two of its three premises: quotes nocturne cradleSong and heardOnlyWithin cradleSong varaHouse, with ?place bound to varaHouse. The missing fastening is dwelledIn liane varaHouse — that Liane herself lived under that same roof. Until that fact arrives, hadSourceOf liane nocturne cannot be voiced.

meta.hypothesis: Liane composed the nocturne — couldHaveWritten liane nocturne is derived (Rule 3), and quotes nocturne cradleSong plus heardOnlyWithin cradleSong varaHouse are now grounded (two premises of Rule 5 with ?place bound to varaHouse) — but hadSourceOf liane nocturne still requires dwelledIn liane varaHouse, which is absent from the board; draftCorrectedBy nocturne liane is also unestablished. Rule 6 cannot fire without all three limbs.

transcript.text: [The wet shawl is taken away, and the room grows smaller around the lamp. Fresh blotting paper, a clean pane of glass, and a sharpened pencil are set out in silence; the next pressure will be close, patient, and bodily, where a mark must answer to a hand.]

phase.name: Act 4
phase.intent: [The wet shawl is taken away, and the room grows smaller around the lamp. Fresh blotting paper, a clean pane of glass, and a sharpened pencil are set out in silence; the next pressure will be close, patient, and bodily, where a mark must answer to a hand.]

transcript.text: Keep the Vara walls fixed, but do not yet give them a tenant. If the cradle-song was kept only inside that house, what separate kind of fact must the room still show about a candidate before Rule 5 may award source?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut: the last two turns are not the same device, though the draft’s question sits awkwardly beside a reconstruction that lists no missing premises.

transcript.text: Rule 5 requires three premises together: the work quotes the tune, the tune was heard only within those walls, and the candidate dwelled within those walls. The first two are now on my board. What the room must still show is a residence fact — proof that a named person actually lived inside Vara House. Without dwelledIn ?x varaHouse for some candidate, the confinement of the cradle-song to those walls awards source to no one.

meta.hypothesis: Liane composed the nocturne — couldHaveWritten liane nocturne is derived (Rule 3), and two of Rule 5's three premises are grounded (quotes nocturne cradleSong, heardOnlyWithin cradleSong varaHouse) — but the residence fact dwelledIn liane varaHouse is absent from the board, so hadSourceOf liane nocturne cannot be derived; draftCorrectedBy nocturne liane is also unestablished. Rule 6 cannot fire without all three limbs.

transcript.text: [The lamp is lowered until its circle catches only the bare edge of the glass and the waiting pencil. Outside the archive door, rain ticks steadily from the eaves, while the room holds its breath over the untouched page.]

Then the source square is still a lock with one tooth missing. The nocturne quotes the cradle-song; Odile has fixed that song inside the Vara house. What kind of fact, and not yet whose, must meet those walls before Rule 5 can yield a source?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut is present, though the draft treats a premise as still missing while its reconstruction lists no missing premise.

transcript.text: The room must show a residence fact — dwelledIn ?x varaHouse for some named candidate. The tune's confinement to those walls is fixed; what remains open is whether any person of interest actually lived inside them. Until that predicate arrives, Rule 5 holds its source in reserve regardless of who is proposed.

meta.hypothesis: Liane composed the nocturne — couldHaveWritten liane nocturne is derived (Rule 3), and two of Rule 5's three premises are grounded (quotes nocturne cradleSong, heardOnlyWithin cradleSong varaHouse) — but the residence fact dwelledIn liane varaHouse is absent from the board, so hadSourceOf liane nocturne cannot be derived; draftCorrectedBy nocturne liane is also unestablished. Rule 6 cannot fire without all three limbs.

transcript.text: [A patron's letter is brought in with its seal broken, and the sentence is read aloud to the whole room: "And yet — Vess summered at the Vara house for years, the patron's own guest; whatever was sung under that roof, he will have heard." The lamp gives a small hard gleam on the clean pane of glass.]

The guest-letter gives Vess a path to the song, not a path to the room. Set it beside the concert bills: if he heard what the Vara house kept, but was away through the flood winter, which square of Rule 6 remains barred to him?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut: the last two declared figures are erotema and anaphora, and the draft's erotema does not make three in a row.

transcript.text: The guest-letter now grounds dwelledIn vess varaHouse, and Rule 5 yields hadSourceOf vess nocturne. But awayDuring vess floodWinter means Rule 3 cannot supply couldHaveWritten — that square of Rule 6 stays barred to him. The source is in his hand; the opportunity is not. Rule 6 demands all three in one fist, and Vess holds only one.

transcript.text: Then keep the verdict in the law's order, not in the room's old temptation: opportunity, source, correcting hand. Which of those three did the final comparison close, and why can style no longer overrule the three conjuncts now standing together?

phase.name: Act 7

transcript.text: The last page stays open because a verdict becomes a duty when it can be audited. If another hand reaches first for style, what three grounded entries must the archive keep together so the conclusion remains the law's yield and not the room's preference?

[The papers are not moved. A clerk replaces the cover on the inkstand, and the silence that follows has the shape of a record waiting to be copied without ornament.]
Then the copy must be bare enough to be checked. When the record names Liane, should it carry a flourish of style, or the three audit entries: opportunity, source, and correcting hand?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut: the sequence is anaphora, erotema, erotema, so the draft has not made three of one device in a row.

transcript.text: Three entries, not flourish. HadSourceOf is clean from the board: quotes at seven, heardOnlyWithin at eight, dwelledIn at ten — Rule Five entire. The correcting hand is grounded at index twelve. CouldHaveWritten requires writtenDuring, and the watermark source has slipped — but the stock-period fact at index five remains, and atConservatoryDuring liane floodWinter stands directly at index six; the chain holds, all three squares close, and the record may be copied bare: Liane composed the nocturne.
[The clerk draws a fresh sheet forward and leaves the heading blank. Beyond the high windows, the water in the courtyard lies still, holding the conservatory's reflection without improving it.]

Restore the first line before the clerk copies the last: every leaf of the nocturne bears the heron watermark, the Brell mill's mark. With that mark joined to the stock-book's flood-winter limit, and Liane joined to that winter by witness and ledger, how should the record state the opportunity square before it names source and hand?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut: the last two turns used erotema, but this draft shifts to anaphora; it also attends to the reconstructed missing premise rather than ignoring it.

transcript.text: The opportunity square stated bare: watermark at five plus stock-limit at six yield writtenDuring nocturne floodWinter by Rule One. The ledger entry at twelve yields atConservatoryDuring liane floodWinter by Rule Four — that same fact stands independently at index seven, so the ground is doubled. Rule Three joins those two: couldHaveWritten liane nocturne. Source closes by Rule Five from eight, nine, and eleven; hand closes at thirteen; Rule Six fires and the record names Liane.

acts.brief: [The flooded archive gives back only damp silence, and the conservatory room gathers around an unsigned page as if around a verdict not yet spoken. Lamplight trembles over the table; outside, rain works at the windows.]

[The swollen folio lies unopened in the lamplight, its warped cover casting a narrow shadow across the unsigned page. The room shifts from verdict to reckoning; rain hardens at the windows as the next act gathers around time itself.]

[The papers are gathered into separate piles under the lamp: one for likeness, one for time, one for absence. Beyond the rain-dark windows, the archive seems to listen for another kind of testimony, softer than ledgers and harder to dismiss.]

[The wet shawl is taken away, and the room grows smaller around the lamp. Fresh blotting paper, a clean pane of glass, and a sharpened pencil are set out in silence; the next pressure will be close, patient, and bodily, where a mark must answer to a hand.]

[The pencil is turned once beside the clean glass, its point dark against the blotting paper. The room holds its breath at the edge of recognition, no longer widening the question but narrowing it toward the mark that must be seen.]
[The glass is lifted away, leaving only the silence it made visible. Rain works softly at the high windows, and the room changes from pursuit to reckoning.]
[The room does not answer at once. The last page is left open on the table while the rain withdraws from the windows, and what remains is not pursuit but the weight of custody.]
